perturbation.original_text: 1.21 "Products" means recycled or upcycled PET resins.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Products" has been broadened from "recycled or upcycled PET resins" to simply "materials." This creates an in-text contradiction if other clauses in the agreement specify that "Products" refers to PET resins, for example, the original version specifies: Loop shall have the sole and exclusive right to enter into agreements for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter, and the exclusive right to set the price and quantity to be supplied under each Loop Sales Contract and other terms and conditions as determined by Loop in its commercially reasonable discretion. This is now contradicted since Loop now has the right to sell ANY materials, and not the originally specified recycled PET resins, which changes the very nature of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 Loop Sales Contracts. As between the Parties, subject to Section 2.7, Loop shall have the sole and exclusive right to enter into agreements for the sale of Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter, and the exclusive right to set the price and quantity to be supplied under each Loop Sales Contract and other terms and conditions as determined by Loop in its commercially reasonable discretion.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined "Products" as "recycled or upcycled PET resins." The changed text broadens this definition to "materials." This creates a contradiction with Section 2.1, which grants Loop the exclusive right to sell Loop-branded Products or any Products produced using Licensed Subject Matter. Since "Products" originally referred specifically to recycled or upcycled PET resins, Section 2.1 originally granted Loop the exclusive right to sell PET resins. Now that Products has been broadened to 'materials', this means that Loop now has the rights to sell ANY material, which changes the fundamental understanding of the agreement and is a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.10 "Contract Eligibility Framework" means, with respect to any Proposed Contract, those criteria set forth Exhibit B.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the definition of "Contract Eligibility Framework" while still referencing it within the agreement creates ambiguity. Section 2.2 refers to this framework, stating the criteria must be met for a contract to be 'Eligible.' Without a defined framework, determining contract eligibility becomes subjective, creating an in-text contradiction. The contract still references to criteria's to establish whether the Proposed Contract meets in the Contract Eligibility Framework, but the section has been deleted, creating an in-text contradiction. The change makes enforcements ambiguous since the specific criteria are not defined, enforcement becomes arbitrary.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2 Proposed Contracts and Proposed Contract Notice. Loop shall give Joint Venture Company prompt written notice, in the form of Exhibit A attached to this Agreement, [***] and (b) pursuant to which Loop offers to assign all of its rights and obligations under such Loop Sales Contract to Joint Venture Company (each such notice, a "Proposed Contract Notice"). Along with each Proposed Contract Notice, Loop shall deliver to Joint Venture Company a complete copy of the executed Loop Sales Contract (the "Proposed Contract"). The Proposed Contract Notice shall contain information necessary to establish whether the Proposed Contract meets the criteria set forth in the Contract Eligibility Framework. Each such Proposed Contract that (i) meets the criteria set forth in the Contract Eligibility Framework and (ii) contains no other terms or conditions other than those set forth in the Contract Eligibility Framework or any form of Loop Sales Contract mutually agreed in writing to be acceptable by the Parties, shall be an "Eligible Contract." Each such Proposed Contract that does not qualify as an Eligible Contract shall be a "Considered Contract."
perturbation.justification: The removal of the definition of "Contract Eligibility Framework" in section 1.10 directly contradicts section 2.2, which explicitly references this framework to determine whether a Proposed Contract is an "Eligible Contract." Without a defined framework, the criteria for eligibility are undefined, creating ambiguity and making the enforcement of the agreement subjective and potentially arbitrary. This aligns with the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition as essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 LIMITATION OF LIABILITY. IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER UNDER THIS AGREEMENT OR OTHERWISE FOR CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, EXEMPLARY, STATUTORY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT. JOINT VENTURE COMPANY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY TO THE LOOP PARTIES WITH RESPECT TO A TRANSFERRED CONTRACT, IN ALL CIRCUMSTANCES AND REGARDLESS OF THE THEORY OF RECOVERY, SHALL BE LIMITED TO THE AMOUNT OF ACTUAL, DIRECT, AND DOCUMENTED DAMAGES THE APPLICABLE AUTHORIZED CUSTOMER ACTUALLY RECOVERS FROM THE LOOP PARTIES UNDER THE TRANSFERRED CONTRACT BASED SOLELY ON JOINT VENTURE COMPANY'S BREACH OF SUCH TRANSFERRED CONTRACT. JOINT VENTURE COMPANY SHALL NOT HAVE ANY LIABILITY TO ANY LOOP PARTY WITH RESPECT TO ANY ELIGIBLE CONTRACT THAT DOES NOT BECOME A TRANSFERRED CONTRACT AS PROVIDED IN SECTION 2.3. NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, NONE OF THE FOREGOING LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 3.1 SHALL BE AVAILABLE TO EITHER PARTY WITH RESPECT TO A BREACH OF SECTION 4, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, OR FOR CLAIMS TO THE EXTENT THEY ARISE OUT OF A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, FRAUD, OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 LIMITATION OF LIABILITY. IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER UNDER THIS AGREEMENT OR OTHERWISE FOR CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, EXEMPLARY, STATUTORY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT. JOINT VENTURE COMPANY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY TO THE LOOP PARTIES WITH RESPECT TO A TRANSFERRED CONTRACT, IN ALL CIRCUMSTANCES AND REGARDLESS OF THE THEORY OF RECOVERY, SHALL BE LIMITED TO THE AMOUNT OF ACTUAL, DIRECT, AND DOCUMENTED DAMAGES THE APPLICABLE AUTHORIZED CUSTOMER ACTUALLY RECOVERS FROM THE LOOP PARTIES UNDER THE TRANSFERRED CONTRACT BASED SOLELY ON JOINT VENTURE COMPANY'S BREACH OF SUCH TRANSFERRED CONTRACT. JOINT VENTURE COMPANY SHALL NOT HAVE ANY LIABILITY TO ANY LOOP PARTY WITH RESPECT TO ANY ELIGIBLE CONTRACT THAT DOES NOT BECOME A TRANSFERRED CONTRACT AS PROVIDED IN SECTION 2.3.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause "NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, NONE OF THE FOREGOING LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 3.1 SHALL BE AVAILABLE TO EITHER PARTY WITH RESPECT TO A BREACH OF SECTION 4, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, OR FOR CLAIMS TO THE EXTENT THEY ARISE OUT OF A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, FRAUD, OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT." introduces an in-text contradiction. This clause previously exempted certain breaches (confidentiality, gross negligence, fraud, etc.) from the liability limitations. Now, those limitations apply EVEN to breaches of confidentiality or acts of gross negligence. This contradicts the intent and protection afforded by Section 4 (Confidential Information) and creates an internal inconsistency because the contract defines specific obligation regarding Confidential Information but limits recourse for breaching those obligations, nullifying that protection in practice.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.1 Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information" means any information disclosed by one Party to the other (i) prior to the date of this Agreement but with respect to the subject matter of this Agreement, or (ii) pursuant to this Agreement, in each case which is in written, graphic, machine readable or other tangible form and is marked "Confidential," "Proprietary" or in some other manner to indicate its confidential nature. Confidential Information may also include oral information disclosed by one Party to the other pursuant to this Agreement, pro-vided that such information is designated as confidential at the time of disclosure and reduced to a written summary by the disclosing Party, within thirty (30) days after its oral disclosure, which is marked in a manner to indicate its confidential nature and delivered to the receiving party.
perturbation.justification: The removal of the clause "NOTWITHSTANDING THE FOREGOING, NONE OF THE FOREGOING LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY SET FORTH IN THIS SECTION 3.1 SHALL BE AVAILABLE TO EITHER PARTY WITH RESPECT TO A BREACH OF SECTION 4, CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, OR FOR CLAIMS TO THE EXTENT THEY ARISE OUT OF A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, FRAUD, OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT." directly contradicts Section 4.1, which defines Confidential Information. Originally, breaches of confidentiality were explicitly exempt from the liability limitations in Section 3.1. Now, with the clause removed, the liability limitations apply even to breaches of confidentiality, effectively weakening or nullifying the protection afforded to Confidential Information under Section 4.1. This creates a direct conflict because Section 4.1 establishes a right to protect confidential information, while the modified Section 3.1 limits recourse for violating that right, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
